narrative: SAIL improved its FY2024 ESG benchmark position materially, recording 38 rank-one outcomes, 20 rank-two outcomes and 24 rank-four outcomes. Its standalone boundary included five integrated steel plants, three special steel plants, multiple units and mines, making it a broad domestic operating footprint rather than a narrow corporate entity. Environmentally, SAIL remained constrained by integrated steelmaking and legacy asset intensity. It ranked last on total energy, physical energy intensity, energy intensity per rupee and employee fatalities, and remained weaker than Jindal and JSW on GHG operational metrics. However, it improved its air-emissions comparability and ranked second on SOx and NOx. It also had the lowest total waste disposal and led several waste categories. Socially, SAIL’s profile was split: it led employee LTIFR and POSH upheld complaints, and had the strongest wellbeing expenditure intensity at 1.46% of revenue, but lagged on training breadth and had weaker Board diversity. Governance was a relative strength. SAIL was the cleanest RPT concentration benchmark across investments, loans/advances, purchases and sales, reflecting its PSU structure and more internalised operating model. Strategically, FY2024 shows SAIL as improving and governance-stable, with strong welfare and procurement characteristics, but still materially exposed to energy intensity, carbon intensity and training-system gaps.
